title: Onion bhaji
subtitle: Crisp, spiced onion fritters in chickpea batter
excerpt: Thinly sliced onions bound together in a spiced chickpea flour batter and deep-fried until golden and crisp outside with a soft, sweet onion interior. The batter holds the onions together but is not thick, each bhaji is more onion than batter. The classic starter of the British Indian restaurant, paired with mint chutney.
sourceNotes: Onion bhaji (pakora in Punjabi cooking) is a deep-fried snack found across South Asia in various forms. The British curry-house version uses a gram flour (chickpea flour) batter and is served as a starter. Documented on British Indian menus from the 1960s onward.

content.text: The batter for bhaji is deliberately thin: it coats the onion slices rather than encasing them in a thick layer. Mix the chickpea flour with just enough water to bring it together into a paste that coats, not pours. The onions themselves release moisture as they sit in the salt and batter, which thins the mixture further over time, prepare and fry quickly, or the batter loosens too much.

Oil temperature matters. At 180°C the batter sets quickly on the outside and the bhaji holds its shape; below this temperature the batter absorbs oil before it sets and the result is greasy rather than crisp. A kitchen thermometer removes guesswork. If no thermometer is available, drop a small piece of batter into the oil. It should sizzle immediately and rise to the surface.

Combine the {{gram-flour}}, {{turmeric}}, {{chilli-powder}}, {{cumin-seeds}}, and {{sea-salt-fine}} in a bowl. Add enough cold water (about 70 to 80 ml) to make a thick paste that coats a spoon. Add the sliced {{onion}}, {{chilli-green}}, and {{coriander}} and mix well so every onion strand is coated. Heat the {{vegetable-oil}} in a deep pan to 180°C. Working in batches, drop heaped spoonfuls of the onion mixture into the oil. Fry for 3 to 4 minutes per batch, turning once, until golden and crisp. Remove with a slotted spoon and drain on paper. Serve immediately.

items.fix: Check oil temperature is at 180°C before adding the first batch. If the batter is too thin, add another tablespoon of gram flour and mix. The batter should hold the onion strands together visibly in the spoon before they go in
items.cause: Batter too thin or oil not hot enough
items.symptom: Bhajis fall apart in the oil

items.fix: The batter must hit hot oil. Allow the oil to come back to temperature between batches, adding too many bhajis at once drops the temperature
items.symptom: Greasy and soft rather than crisp

items.fix: Keep bhajis to a heaped-tablespoon size. If the outside is colouring too fast, reduce the oil temperature slightly
items.cause: Too large or oil too hot (surface sets before inside cooks)

items.fix: Make the batter thicker than you think you need, the onions release moisture as they sit, thinning it. Fry quickly after mixing

content.text: Onion bhaji is the most ordered starter in the British Indian restaurant tradition. It works because the mix is simple and reliable: crisp batter, sweet onion centre, green chilli heat, fresh coriander. Serve with tamarind chutney or mint raita while you wait for the mains. At home it asks for care with the oil temperature, but it is one of the easier deep-frying jobs.
